expectations.text: States the fundamental difference correctly: AI RMF is voluntary risk guidance; the EU AI Act is binding law with penalties, conformity assessment, and CE marking — RMF alignment alone never satisfies the AI Act
expectations.evidence: 'the AI RMF is voluntary and outcome-based, not a compliance checklist, while the EU AI Act is a binding legal regulation with specific mandatory deliverables, deadlines, and penalties. If you implement AI RMF alone, you will build strong risk management practice but will still have concrete compliance gaps' plus table row 'Enforcement: None — self-directed' vs 'Market surveillance authorities...fines up to €35M or 7%...' and explicit CE marking/conformity assessment gaps listed.

expectations.text: Provides sensible construct mappings: GOVERN ↔ QMS Art. 17 / accountability; MAP ↔ risk classification + intended-purpose documentation; MEASURE ↔ Art. 15 accuracy/robustness testing + Art. 9 risk management evidence; MANAGE ↔ Art. 9 lifecycle risk management + post-market monitoring Art. 72
expectations.evidence: Response maps GOVERN 1 to Art. 9, GOVERN 2/3 to Art. 16/26, MAP 1/3 to Art. 9(2), MEASURE 2 to Art. 10/15, MEASURE 3 to Art. 72, MANAGE 3 to Art. 73. It does cover MEASURE↔Art.15 and MANAGE↔Art.72(partial, listed under MEASURE 3 not MANAGE) but never maps GOVERN to Art. 17 (Quality Management System) — Art. 17 is not mentioned anywhere in the response. The QMS/Art.17 linkage specifically requested is missing, so the mapping is not fully as specified.

expectations.text: Identifies concrete AI Act obligations with no RMF equivalent: conformity assessment/CE marking, EU database registration, technical documentation per Annex IV, serious-incident reporting, FRIA (Art. 27) where applicable, prohibited-practice screening
expectations.evidence: Numbered gap list explicitly covers: '2. Conformity assessment procedure (Art. 43)', '3. CE marking and EU database registration (Art. 48, Art. 71)', '4. Annex IV technical documentation format', '6. Serious incident reporting...(Art. 73)', '5. Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment (Art. 27)', '7. Prohibited practices screening (Art. 5)' — all six named gaps present with article citations.

expectations.text: Notes risk-tiering difference: AI Act's fixed legal tiers (prohibited/high/limited/minimal + GPAI) vs RMF's contextual risk assessment
expectations.evidence: Table row: 'Scope | Any AI system, any organization, self-scoped | Risk-tiered (unacceptable/high-risk/limited/minimal), applies to in-scope providers & deployers with EU nexus' and gap #8 references GPAI obligations (Art. 53-55) as a separate category, contrasting with RMF's non-tiered, contextual approach implied throughout (e.g., 'AI RMF doesn't tell you whether your system is high-risk').

expectations.text: Recommends a practical integration: use RMF processes as the management-system engine, then overlay AI Act artifact requirements — one programme, two outputs
expectations.evidence: 'Recommended Approach' section: 'Use AI RMF GOVERN as your organizational foundation... Run Art. 5 prohibited-practice screening... Use MAP/MEASURE to generate the technical content, then reformat into Annex IV structure... Add the EU AI Act-specific mandatory artifacts... as parallel workstreams' — precisely the 'one programme, two outputs' integration model requested.
